schools.summary: Gill: the LORD spake to Moses about the same time, and many laws in this chapter were given before and are here repeated.

Henry: Moses was ordered to deliver the summary of the laws to all the congregation, not to Aaron and his sons only, for all were concerned to know their duty; religion that boasts of ignorance as its mother cannot be of God.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the law of holiness proclaimed to all
against a religion built on ignorance

schools.summary: Gill: all the congregation are addressed, including the proselytes; this section contains all the ten commandments with various judicial and ceremonial laws, the more substantial parts of the body of the law depending on it.
Keil & Delitzsch: the keynote of the chapter is holiness of life, the people to be holy because the LORD their God is holy.

themes: Ye shall be holy: for I the LORD your God am holy
the holiness of God the pattern of his people's
the Apostle taking up the word (1 Pet. 1:16)

schools.summary: Gill: this respects the fifth command, the first with promise; the fear is filial, not servile, joined with love, esteem, reverence, and ready obedience; even the heathen, next to honouring God, exhorted to reverence parents.
Keil & Delitzsch: every man to fear his mother and his father and keep the LORD's sabbaths, joining the duties to man and to God.

themes: fear every man his mother, and his father
keep my sabbaths
the fifth and fourth commandments joined

schools.summary: Gill: turn not from the one true and living God to idols, which are 'things of nought,' nothing in the world, of no worth or use; even to look at them attentively or have respect to them in the heart was forbidden.
Keil & Delitzsch: turn not unto idols nor make molten gods, the LORD their God alone to be worshipped.

themes: Turn ye not unto idols, nor make... molten gods
idols are nothing (1 Cor. 8:4)
the worship of the one true God

schools.summary: Gill: the voluntary peace offering is meant, one of the three sorts (thanksgiving, vow, freewill offering), offered at a man's own will.

Keil & Delitzsch: true fidelity to Jehovah was shown in keeping the holiness of the sacrificial flesh, the command of Lev. 7:15-18 repeated that none be eaten on the third day, on pain of extermination.

themes: offer a sacrifice of peace offerings... at your own will
the holiness of the sacrifice preserved
fidelity to the LORD in worship

schools.summary: Gill: it was best to eat it all the same day, the remainder eaten on the morrow but no longer; this marks the vow or freewill peace offering.
Keil & Delitzsch: vv.6-8 repeat the command that none remain to the third day, what is left to be burned in the fire.

themes: eaten the same day... and on the morrow
the third day it shall be burnt
the holy flesh kept from becoming common

schools.summary: Gill: 'in eating be eaten' — any least bit eaten on the third day; it is abominable and shall not be accepted.
Clarke and Ellicott: refer to the fuller statement of the law at Lev. 7:18, the offering rejected if eaten on the third day.

themes: if it be eaten... on the third day, it is abominable
it shall not be accepted
obedience in the manner of worship

schools.summary: Gill: every one that eats it bears his iniquity, is chargeable with sin and pronounced guilty, the punishment being cutting off.
Ellicott: the formula 'cut off from his people' threatens death by excision and should be rendered uniformly; he has profaned the hallowed thing of the LORD.

themes: every one that eateth it shall bear his iniquity
he hath profaned the hallowed thing of the LORD
cut off from among his people

schools.summary: Gill: when they reaped the harvest of Canaan, barley or wheat, the corner was not to be wholly reaped.
Keil & Delitzsch: not to reap the field to the extreme edge, nor gather the ears left, but leave them for the distressed and the foreigner that he might share.

themes: thou shalt not wholly reap the corners of thy field
neither... the gleanings
the portion left for the poor (Ruth 2:2)

schools.summary: Gill: the small 'infant' clusters were not to be cut off but left for the poor; if the whole vine were such, it all belonged to them.
Keil & Delitzsch: not to glean the vineyard nor gather the fallen grapes and olives, but leave them for the poor and the stranger.

themes: thou shalt not glean thy vineyard
leave them for the poor and stranger
charity grounded in 'I am the LORD'

schools.summary: Gill: refers to the eighth command; the plural takes in not the actual thief only but him who sees and is silent, who is even as the thief.
Keil & Delitzsch: the Israelites were not to steal, nor deny a deposit, nor lie one to another.

themes: Ye shall not steal, neither deal falsely, neither lie
honesty in deed and word
the sins the renewed man puts off (Eph. 4:25)

schools.summary: Gill: not to swear by God's name to a falsehood; stealing leads to false dealing, then lying, then false swearing; the law reaches every name and epithet of God, not the proper name only, that none be used to seal a lie.

themes: ye shall not swear by my name falsely
neither... profane the name of thy God
the third command and the holy name (Exod. 20:7)

schools.summary: Gill: not to defraud in buying and selling, in retaining wages, in refusing to return a trust or repay a loan; nor to rob openly by force; the hired man's wages not to abide overnight.
Ellicott: here oppression by fraud and oppression by violence are both forbidden.

themes: Thou shalt not defraud thy neighbour, neither rob him
the wages... shall not abide... all night
the cry of the withheld wage (Jas. 5:4)

schools.summary: Gill: there is something mean and base as well as wicked in cursing the deaf, who cannot hear or reply; the law reaches any absent person equally unable to answer; the fear of God is the guard, since the helpless cannot defend themselves.

themes: Thou shalt not curse the deaf, nor put a stumblingblock before the blind
but shalt fear thy God
mercy to the helpless, guarded by the fear of God

schools.summary: Gill: said of judges and witnesses, that none bear false witness nor pronounce an unrighteous sentence, justifying the wicked and condemning the righteous.

Keil & Delitzsch: in the administration of justice they were to do no unrighteousness, neither respecting the person of the poor nor honouring the person of the mighty, but judging the neighbour in righteousness.

themes: Ye shall do no unrighteousness in judgment
not... the poor, nor... the mighty
God is no respecter of persons (Acts 10:34)

schools.summary: Gill: the word for talebearer is a peddler who goes house to house; such a man carries stories to others' hurt to curry favour for himself, and is detestable.
Keil & Delitzsch: not to go about as a calumniator among the people, nor to stand against the blood of the neighbour, seeking his life by false witness.

themes: Thou shalt not go up and down as a talebearer
neither... stand against the blood of thy neighbour
the neighbour's name and life guarded

schools.summary: Gill: hatred in the heart, though never shown, breaks the sixth command; to let a brother go on in sin without reproof is by interpretation a hatred of him.
Keil & Delitzsch: not to cherish hatred in the heart, but in any wise to rebuke the neighbour and not bear sin on his account by silence.

themes: Thou shalt not hate thy brother in thine heart
thou shalt in any wise rebuke thy neighbour
love that rebukes, not flatters

schools.summary: Gill: avenging is returning ill for ill; the grudge illustrated by refusing a tool to one who once refused you; over against both stands the command to love the neighbour as thyself.
Keil & Delitzsch: not to avenge nor bear a grudge against the children of one's people, but to love the neighbour as thyself, the whole duty to man gathered into one word.

themes: thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself
not avenge, nor bear any grudge
the second great commandment (Matt. 22:39; Rom. 13:9)

schools.summary: Gill: distinguishes the foregoing moral laws, planted in the heart, from these of positive institution, the decree of the King, whose reasons are not so plain.
Keil & Delitzsch: not to let cattle gender with a diverse kind, nor sow the field with mingled seed, nor wear a garment of linen and woollen mingled.

themes: Thou shalt not... sow thy field with mingled seed
nor... a garment mingled of linen and woollen
separation and holiness taught by daily life

schools.summary: Gill: the man and woman are of age; the case follows on the law of mingling, of a free man with a bondwoman.

Keil & Delitzsch: even the personal rights of slaves were upheld; the maid, though a slave, was not to be degraded to the condition of personal property; they were not put to death because she was not free.

themes: whosoever lieth carnally with a woman, that is a bondmaid
they shall not be put to death, because she was not free
the personal dignity of the slave upheld

schools.summary: Gill: he shall bring his trespass offering to the priest of the LORD to offer it for him.
Keil & Delitzsch: vv.21-22 appoint the trespass offering, a ram brought to the door of the tabernacle for the sin he had done.

themes: he shall bring his trespass offering... a ram
unto the door of the tabernacle
the sin against God requiring atonement

schools.summary: Gill: the priest offering the sacrifice for him is typical of the atoning sacrifice of Christ.
Ellicott: having offered the trespass offering by the prescribed ritual, the sinner was expiated for his sin and declared free by the officiating priest (cf. Lev. 4:20, 26).

themes: the priest shall make an atonement for him
the sin... shall be forgiven him
a type of the atonement of Christ (1 John 1:9)

schools.summary: Gill: when they came into the land of Canaan and planted trees for food, the fruit was counted uncircumcised for three years.
Clarke: sees no need for a mystical meaning — the fruit of a young tree, not yet mature, is unwholesome, its juices not sufficiently elaborated; eaten only from the fifth year.

debates.question: Is the three-year law natural or sacramental?

sides.position: Natural — the immature fruit is poor and unwholesome (Clarke)
Sacred — the fruit a reserve, God's claim before the increase is enjoyed

themes: count the fruit thereof as uncircumcised
three years... it shall not be eaten
patience and the priority of God's claim

schools.summary: Gill: the fourth-year fruit was separated and devoted to God, given to the priest or redeemed of him, as the tithe was eaten only at Jerusalem or by redemption.
Keil & Delitzsch: in the fourth year all the fruit was to be holy, to praise the LORD withal.

themes: in the fourth year all the fruit... shall be holy
to praise the LORD withal
the principle of firstfruits (Prov. 3:9)

schools.summary: Gill: in the fifth year, and so in all succeeding years as long as the tree bore, the fruit might be freely eaten.
Ellicott: so far from being losers by waiting till the fifth year, they would be gainers, the tree yielding abundant increase thereafter.

themes: in the fifth year shall ye eat of the fruit
that it may yield... the increase
patient obedience rewarded, never the loser before the LORD

schools.summary: Gill: the eating 'with the blood' is variously read — of flesh not rightly cleared of blood, or of an idolatrous superstitious custom of the Zabians; the enchantment and observing of times are the divinations of the nations, forbidden.

themes: Ye shall not eat any thing with the blood
neither shall ye use enchantment, nor observe times
trusting the LORD, not the diviner

schools.summary: Gill: the rounding is cutting the hair alike at temples, forehead, and behind the ears, as the Arabians did.
Keil & Delitzsch: Herodotus reports the Arab tribes cut their hair so in honour of their god; the practice was an idolatrous rite from which Israel was to be separate.

themes: Ye shall not round the corners of your heads
neither... mar... thy beard
separation from heathen idolatrous custom

schools.summary: Gill: the Amorites, Scythians, Carthaginians, and Romans cut and tore their flesh in mourning; the 'marks' are the tattooed signs the heathen printed on themselves.
Keil & Delitzsch: the cuttings for the dead and printed marks were heathen mourning and idolatrous customs, from which Israel was to be separate.

themes: Ye shall not make any cuttings in your flesh for the dead
nor print any marks upon you
grief and body kept from heathen excess (Deut. 14:1)

schools.summary: Gill: refers to the wicked practice of the Phoenicians and Canaanites, whose women prostituted themselves in the temples of their idols, as at Babylon and Corinth.
Keil & Delitzsch: do not prostitute thy daughter, lest the land fall to whoredom and become full of wickedness.

themes: Do not prostitute thy daughter
lest the land fall to whoredom
the purity of the people and land guarded

schools.summary: Gill: keeping the sabbaths preserves from the idolatry and filthy practices of the Gentiles.

Keil & Delitzsch: the exhortation returns to the chief point, the observance of the LORD's sabbaths and reverence for his sanctuary; where the holy day is kept and reverence lives in the heart, many sins are avoided and life is pervaded by the fear of God.

themes: Ye shall keep my sabbaths, and reverence my sanctuary
the true method of divine worship
true worship the root of a holy life

schools.summary: Gill: the diviners are named from the word for 'bottles,' the ventriloquists whose voice seemed to come from the belly, as the Pythian prophetess and the maid with a spirit of divination; to seek after them was to be defiled by them.

themes: Regard not them that have familiar spirits, neither seek after wizards
to be defiled by them
Israel to seek the LORD alone (Isa. 8:19)

schools.summary: Gill: the Hebrew tradition that a young man rose when an elder came within four cubits and sat again when he passed, that the honour might appear to be for his age; reckoned among the heathen also a sacred duty whose neglect was a crime; the duty bound to the fear of God.

themes: Thou shalt rise up before the hoary head
honour the face of the old man, and fear thy God
reverence of age part of the fear of God

schools.summary: Gill: understood of the proselyte of righteousness or of the gate, or any stranger providentially cast among them, who was not to be vexed.
Keil & Delitzsch: the Israelite was not to oppress the foreigner, as already commanded in Exod. 22:21 and 23:9.

themes: if a stranger sojourn with thee... ye shall not vex him
the defenceless outsider guarded
God the defender of the friendless

schools.summary: Gill: the stranger, especially a proselyte of righteousness, enjoyed the same civil and religious privileges, there being one law for both.
Keil & Delitzsch: the stranger was to be as one born among them, loved as thyself, for they were strangers in the land of Egypt.

themes: the stranger... shall be unto you as one born among you
thou shalt love him as thyself
the royal law stretched to the foreigner (v.18)

schools.summary: Gill: Jarchi notes a measurer is a judge; if he deals deceitfully he perverts judgment, an abomination causing many evils.
Keil & Delitzsch: as a universal rule they were to do no wrong in judgment, or in trade with weights and measures of length and capacity, but keep just scales — the same righteousness as in v.15.

themes: Ye shall do no unrighteousness in judgment, in meteyard, in weight, or in measure
honesty in trade a matter of justice
righteousness in the market as on the bench

schools.summary: Gill: the weights (Hebrew 'stones') were to be neither lighter nor heavier, not one sort to buy with and another to sell; the Jews made them of polished stone, not metals that might rust and lighten.
Keil & Delitzsch: just balances, weights, ephah, and hin were to be kept, grounded in the LORD who brought them out of Egypt.

themes: Just balances, just weights, a just ephah, and a just hin
I am the LORD... which brought you out of... Egypt
integrity a confession of the redeeming God

schools.summary: Gill: gathers the statutes (the determinations of God's sovereign will) and judgments (laws of justice and equity), ceremonial, judicial, and moral, all to be observed and done, grounded in 'I am the LORD.'

themes: observe all my statutes, and all my judgments, and do them
I am the LORD
holiness consisting in obedience to the holy God
